aliases: Rambam (רמב"ם, acronym of Rabbi Moshe ben Maimon)
Abu Imran Musa ibn Ubayd Allah

summary: Maimonides (Moses ben Maimon, the Rambam, 1138-1204), born in Cordoba and active in Fustat (Cairo), was the preeminent medieval Jewish philosopher, jurist, and physician. He composed the Commentary on the Mishnah (including the Thirteen Principles of Faith), the Mishneh Torah (a comprehensive code of Jewish law in fourteen books), and the Guide of the Perplexed, the classic of medieval Jewish philosophy.
description:
Maimonides, Moses ben Maimon, known in the Jewish tradition by the acronym Rambam (Rabbi Moshe ben Maimon) and in Arabic as Musa ibn Maymun, was born in Cordoba in Muslim Spain and, after his family's departure from Spain and a period in North Africa, settled in Fustat (Old Cairo) in Egypt, where he served as a leader of the Jewish community and as a physician. He is generally regarded in the scholarship as the preeminent Jewish thinker of the medieval period, at once a codifier of Jewish law (halakha), a philosopher, and a working physician whose medical writings circulated widely.

Three works stand at the centre of his legacy. The Commentary on the Mishnah, written in Judeo-Arabic, includes two celebrated set-pieces: the introduction to the tenth chapter of tractate Sanhedrin (Perek Helek), which sets out his Thirteen Principles of Faith (later paraphrased in the liturgical Ani Ma'amin and Yigdal), and the eight chapters (Shemonah Perakim) on ethics prefacing his commentary on tractate Avot. The Mishneh Torah ('Repetition of the Torah'), written in clear Mishnaic Hebrew, is a comprehensive code of the whole of Jewish law arranged systematically into fourteen books, from which it also bears the name Sefer ha-Yad or 'Yad ha-Hazakah' (the numerical value of yad being fourteen); its opening book, the Book of Knowledge (Sefer ha-Madda), begins with the Laws of the Foundations of the Torah (Hilchot Yesodei ha-Torah), whose first chapter states the knowledge of God's existence and unity as the foundational commandment. Isadore Twersky's study reads the Mishneh Torah as a work that fuses law and philosophy into a single codificatory vision. The Guide of the Perplexed (Moreh Nevukhim), also in Judeo-Arabic, is the classic of medieval Jewish philosophy: addressed to a reader perplexed by the apparent conflict between the Torah and philosophy, it develops a negative theology (the via negativa, holding that God is known only through the negation of attributes rather than by positive predication), an account of prophecy, and a treatment of divine providence, engaging throughout with Aristotle and with the Muslim philosophers (the falasifa) al-Farabi and Ibn Sina, whom Maimonides read closely.

Maimonides' rationalism, and in particular the Guide and his account of resurrection and the world's createdness, provoked sustained controversy after his death, the so-called Maimonidean controversy of the thirteenth century, in which rabbinic authorities in Provence, Spain, and northern France divided over whether his philosophical program was compatible with traditional Torah study; the dispute is recorded here as a historical disagreement with defenders and critics on both sides, not adjudicated. Alongside the controversy stands his enduring authority: the epitaph 'from Moses to Moses there was none like Moses' expresses the tradition's estimate of his stature. Moshe Halbertal's biography treats his life, his three great books, and his philosophical outlook together, and Menachem Kellner's study traces how the Thirteen Principles introduced systematic dogma into Jewish thought. He is presented here on Judaism's own terms, with the philosophical and historical scholarship recorded alongside as attributed sources.
editorial_notes: This entry presents Maimonides on Judaism's own terms as jurist, philosopher, and physician (neutrality #1, #4, #6), and reports the Maimonidean controversy as a historical dispute with both sides attributed and not adjudicated (neutrality #1-#2): the entry names the disagreement and its parties without ruling on whether his rationalism was compatible with traditional Torah study. His engagement with Islamic philosophy (al-Farabi, Ibn Sina, and Aristotle as read through the falasifa) is stated factually per neutrality #10-#11, as intellectual influence, without ranking traditions. Sources triangulate three lenses per neutrality #8-9: (a) academic historians of Maimonides and his code (Twersky; Davidson); (b) the primary texts, open and auditable on Sefaria (the Mishneh Torah's Hilchot Yesodei ha-Torah, read line-by-line, and the Guide in Pines's scholarly translation); and (c) contemporary Jewish scholars (Halbertal; Kellner). No encyclopedias (the Encyclopaedia Judaica, the Jewish Encyclopedia, Chabad.org, My Jewish Learning), no Wikipedia, no tertiary sources. Confidence: the Mishneh Torah, Foundations of the Torah 1:1-7 on Sefaria is rated high because it was read line-by-line for this entry, with the opening halakha quoted verbatim; the Pines translation of the Guide is rated medium (verified to exist and be on-topic, the negative-theology and prophecy material not read line-by-line here); the secondary monographs (Twersky, Davidson, Halbertal, Kellner) are medium, verified via publisher and library records. The Thirteen Principles are given as summarized in Kellner's monograph rather than from a tertiary paraphrase. Relation targets: aristotle exists (data/figures/philosophy/aristotle.json) and ibn-sina exists (data/figures/islam/ibn-sina.json); al-farabi, mishnah, talmud, torah, tanakh, rabbinic-judaism, halakha, and mashiach are referenced as relation targets, of which mishnah, torah, tanakh, and rabbinic-judaism exist and al-farabi, halakha, and mashiach are dangling forward references to not-yet-created entities.

lifespan.note: 1138-1204 CE (birth also reported as 1135 in older scholarship); born in Cordoba in Muslim Spain, died in Fustat, Egypt, and buried in Tiberias by tradition. The year 1138 follows the corrected dating adopted in recent scholarship (Davidson, Halbertal).

relations.note: The Guide of the Perplexed engages Aristotelian philosophy throughout, seeking to reconcile it with the Torah; Maimonides read Aristotle largely through the mediation of the Muslim philosophers. The aristotle entry exists.

Maimonides held al-Farabi in high regard as a philosopher and drew on his logic and political philosophy; stated factually as intellectual influence (neutrality #10-#11). Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.
Maimonides read Ibn Sina (Avicenna) among the falasifa and engaged Avicennan metaphysics in the Guide; stated factually as intellectual engagement. The ibn-sina entry exists (islam).
His Commentary on the Mishnah (in Judeo-Arabic) includes the Thirteen Principles of Faith (introduction to Perek Helek) and the Shemonah Perakim on ethics. The mishnah entry exists.
The Mishneh Torah codifies the law of the Talmud into a comprehensive systematic code; his Commentary on the Mishnah and legal writings rest on the talmudic corpus. The talmud entry exists.

The Mishneh Torah is a comprehensive code of Jewish law (halakha) in fourteen books, one of the most influential halakhic works in the tradition. Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.
The Mishneh Torah ('Repetition of the Torah') codifies the whole law derived from the Torah, and the Guide interprets the relation of the Torah to philosophy. The torah entry exists.

The twelfth of the Thirteen Principles affirms the coming of the Messiah (mashiach); Maimonides treats the messianic age in the Mishneh Torah (Hilchot Melakhim). Dangling forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.
As codifier of the law and a central authority of the medieval tradition, Maimonides stands within rabbinic Judaism, building on the Mishnah and Talmud. The rabbinic-judaism entry exists.

sources.citation: Mishneh Torah, Foundations of the Torah (Hilchot Yesodei ha-Torah), chapter 1, halachot 1-7, open Hebrew and English text on Sefaria (Touger translation). Cited as the primary text for Maimonides' opening statement that 'the foundation of all foundations and the pillar of wisdom is to know that there is a Primary Being who brought into being all existence' (1:1), that this knowledge is a positive commandment implied by Exodus 20:2 (1:6), and that 'this God is one ... unified in a manner which surpasses any unity that is found in the world' (1:7). Read line-by-line and verified against the text.
sources.license: Hebrew original in the public domain; Sefaria's English translation under a Creative Commons license. Halachot 1:1-7 read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Maimonides, Moses. The Guide of the Perplexed. Translated with an introduction and notes by Shlomo Pines, with an introductory essay by Leo Strauss. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1963. The standard scholarly English translation of the Moreh Nevukhim; cited for the negative theology (via negativa), the account of prophecy, and the engagement with Aristotle and the falasifa. Verified to exist and be on-topic (University of Chicago Press record); the relevant passages not read line-by-line for this entry.
sources.license: Proprietary translation of a classical text; cited for reference.

sources.citation: Twersky, Isadore. Introduction to the Code of Maimonides (Mishneh Torah). Yale Judaica Series 22. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1980. A literary-historical study of the Mishneh Torah organized around its codificatory form, scope, classification, language, and the fusion of philosophy and law; cited as an academic lens on the code and its character. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Yale University Press record; JSTOR j.ctt1cc2m4n), not read line-by-line.

Davidson, Herbert A. Moses Maimonides: The Man and His Works. Oxford and New York: Oxford University Press, 2005. A comprehensive scholarly biography and survey of Maimonides' rabbinic, philosophical, and medical writings; cited for his life, the corpus of his works, and the corrected dating of his birth. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Oxford University Press record), not read line-by-line.

Halbertal, Moshe. Maimonides: Life and Thought. Translated by Joel Linsider. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2013. A contemporary Jewish scholar's study of Maimonides' life, his three great books (Commentary on the Mishnah, Mishneh Torah, Guide of the Perplexed), and his philosophical outlook on God and the natural order; cited as the contemporary scholarly lens. Verified to exist and be on-topic (Princeton University Press record; 2013 National Jewish Book Award in Scholarship), not read line-by-line.

Kellner, Menachem. Dogma in Medieval Jewish Thought: From Maimonides to Abravanel. Littman Library of Jewish Civilization. Oxford: Oxford University Press for the Littman Library, 1986 (reprinted Portland: Littman Library, 2004). A contemporary Jewish scholar's study of creed-formation in Judaism; cited for Maimonides' introduction of systematic dogma and the content of the Thirteen Principles (as summarized: God's existence, unity, incorporeality, priority, sole worship; prophecy and the superiority of Moses' prophecy; the Torah's revelation and immutability; God's knowledge, reward and punishment; the Messiah; and resurrection). Verified to exist and be on-topic (Littman Library / Oxford record), not read line-by-line.
